description: Procedural criminal law replacing the Criminal Procedure Code (CrPC) from July 1, 2024. Streamlines investigations, trials, and judicial processes.

sections.title: Zero FIR (replaces CrPC 154)
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence, irrespective of the area where the offence is committed, may be given orally or by electronic communication to an officer in charge of a police station.
sections.simplified: Zero FIRs are officially codified. Any police station must register an FIR regardless of jurisdiction. It can also be filed via e-FIR (electronic communication).

sections.title: Forensic Investigation (NEW)
sections.text: On receiving information about an offence punishable with imprisonment of seven years or more, the officer in charge of a police station shall cause the forensic expert to visit the crime scene to collect forensic evidence and cause videography of the process.
sections.simplified: Mandatory forensic investigation and videography at the crime scene for serious offences (7+ years punishment). Ensures evidence is securely recorded and not tampered with.

sections.title: Videography of Search and Seizure (NEW/Updated CrPC 100)
sections.text: The process of conducting search of a place or taking possession of any property, article or thing shall be audio-videographed, preferably by mobile phone.
sections.simplified: Police raids, searches, and seizures must be recorded via audio-video (like on a mobile phone) to prevent planting of evidence.

sections.title: Bail for Undertrials (replaces CrPC 436A)
sections.text: Where a first-time offender has undergone detention for a period extending up to one-third of the maximum period of imprisonment specified for that offence under that law, he shall be released by the Court on bail.
sections.simplified: First-time offenders who have spent 1/3rd of their maximum possible sentence in jail while awaiting trial MUST be released on bail. Others must be released after 1/2 of their maximum sentence.

sections.title: Trial in absentia (NEW)
sections.text: If the accused is an absconder and there is no immediate prospect of arresting him, the court may, after recording reasons, proceed with the trial in the absence of the accused.
sections.simplified: Courts can now conduct a trial and sentence a proclaimed offender (absconder) even if they have fled the country, ensuring justice without delay.

sections.title: Police Custody limits (replaces CrPC 167)
sections.text: The police custody may be authorised beyond the initial fifteen days, in parts or whole, up to a maximum of 60 days or 90 days depending on the offence, but total custody cannot exceed the statutory limits.
sections.simplified: Police can request custody (remand) of the accused in blocks over the entire 60/90 day period, rather than being restricted to only the first 15 days.
